sections.title: What it is
sections.content: Discoloration on stainless steel is a damage issue that needs to be identified correctly before the cleaning method is chosen. On this surface, the issue usually develops because cooking aerosols, iron in water, tool transfer, and high heat alter reflectivity off the oxide layer or leave bonded films that read as uneven color, rainbow, or embedded staining. sections.title: What it is

On this surface, the issue usually develops because painted surfaces streak when cleaner is applied unevenly, moisture is overused, residue is only partially removed, or wipe direction is inconsistent. Some sheen distortion can also mimic streaking. A useful professional clue is that many painted-surface streaks are residue problems, not dirt problems. Technically, a controlled two-cloth, low-moisture process removes residue evenly and reduces the contrast created by lap marks, drying lines, and patchy wipe patterns.
